summary: Poetry exploits our natural desire to relieve sorrow through emotional expression. Because the suffering belongs to fictional others, we see no disgrace in indulging. But this vicarious emotional exercise strengthens our capacity for grief, making it harder to control when real misfortune strikes.
distilled: Indulging in fictional sorrow strengthens the very emotions we need to restrain when real suffering comes.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is a sophisticated psychological argument about emotional contagion and habituation -- "From the evil of other men something of evil is communicated" anticipates modern concerns about media effects on emotional regulation -- The mechanism: vicarious emotional indulgence weakens rational self-control -- Cross-ref: Republic Book III (387d-388d) on the dangers of emotional imitation -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Poetics 1449b (catharsis as counter-argument: tragedy purges these emotions rather than strengthening them) -- Cross-ref: Philebus 47d-50e on mixed pleasures of tragic spectatorship

narrative: "How very true! And does not the same hold also of the ridiculous? There are jests which you would be ashamed to make yourself, and yet on the comic stage, or indeed in private, when you hear them, you are greatly amused by them, and are not at all disgusted at their unseemliness; -- the case of pity is repeated; -- there is a principle in human nature which is disposed to raise a laugh, and this which you once restrained by reason, because you were afraid of being thought a buffoon, is now let out again; and having stimulated the risible faculty at the theatre, you are betrayed unconsciously to yourself into playing the comic poet at home."
refashioned: Socrates: And doesn't the same thing apply to humor? There are jokes you'd be ashamed to make yourself, yet when you hear them on the comic stage or even in private, you're greatly amused and not at all disgusted by their crudeness. The same pattern repeats: there's a natural inclination in us toward laughter, which reason previously restrained for fear of being thought a buffoon. At the theater, that restraint is loosened. And once the comic impulse has been stimulated there, you find yourself unconsciously playing the comedian at home.
elegant: The same principle applies to comedy. Jokes you'd be ashamed to make yourself delight you on stage. Reason once restrained your impulse to clown, but the theater loosens that restraint. Having stimulated your comic faculty there, you unconsciously become the comedian at home.
summary: The corruption extends to comedy: we enjoy jokes on stage that we'd be ashamed to make ourselves. Theater stimulates the comic impulse that reason normally restrains, and once loosened in the theater, this impulse follows us home.
distilled: Theater loosens the restraints reason places on both grief and laughter, and what is indulged in the audience carries over into life.
notes: Speakers: Socrates (with Glaucon assenting) -- This extends the critique from tragedy to comedy -- "Risible faculty" refers to the appetite for crude humor -- The mechanism is identical: theater gives permission, habit forms, restraint weakens -- Cross-ref: Republic Book III (388e-389a) where laughter at heroes was censured -- Cross-ref: Philebus 48a-50b on the psychology of comedy and malice in laughter -- Cross-ref: Laws 816d-817a on regulating comedy in the ideal state

narrative:
"Quite true," he said.

"And the same may be said of lust and anger and all the other affections, of desire and pain and pleasure, which are held to be inseparable from every action -- in all of them poetry feeds and waters the passions instead of drying them up; she lets them rule, although they ought to be controlled, if mankind are ever to increase in happiness and virtue."
refashioned:
Glaucon: Quite true.

Socrates: And the same holds for lust, anger, and every other emotion -- desire, pain, pleasure, all the feelings inseparable from human action. In every case, poetry feeds and waters the passions instead of drying them up. She lets them rule when they should be controlled, if humanity is ever to grow in happiness and virtue.
elegant: What holds for grief and laughter applies to all passions: lust, anger, desire, pain, and pleasure. In every case, poetry feeds and waters the passions instead of drying them up. She lets them rule when they should be controlled -- the opposite of what happiness and virtue require.
summary: The critique generalizes to all emotions. Poetry feeds and waters every passion -- lust, anger, desire, pain, pleasure -- instead of drying them up, letting feelings rule when reason should govern.
distilled: Poetry feeds and waters every passion instead of drying them up, empowering emotion to rule where reason should govern.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- "Feeds and waters the passions" (ardei kai trephei) is one of Plato's most quoted phrases on poetry -- This is the definitive summary of poetry's psychological harm -- The agricultural metaphor (watering/drying) echoes Republic Book VI (485d) on the soul's energies -- Cross-ref: Republic Book IV (441e-442a) on reason's proper rule over the appetites and spirited part -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 246a-254e on the charioteer controlling the horses of the soul

narrative: "Therefore, Glaucon," I said, "whenever you meet with any of the eulogists of Homer declaring that he has been the educator of Hellas, and that he is profitable for education and for the ordering of human things, and that you should take him up again and again and get to know him and regulate your whole life according to him, we may love and honour those who say these things -- they are excellent people, as far as their lights extend; and we are ready to acknowledge that Homer is the greatest of poets and first of tragedy writers; but we must remain firm in our conviction that hymns to the gods and praises of famous men are the only poetry which ought to be admitted into our State. For if you go beyond this and allow the honeyed muse to enter, either in epic or lyric verse, not law and the reason of mankind, which by common consent have ever been deemed best, but pleasure and pain will be the rulers in our State."
refashioned: Socrates: Therefore, Glaucon, whenever you encounter Homer's admirers declaring that he has been the educator of Greece, that his works are profitable for education and the ordering of human affairs, that you should study him thoroughly and regulate your entire life by him -- we can love and respect these people. They mean well, as far as their understanding goes. And we're ready to acknowledge that Homer is the greatest of poets and the foremost writer of tragedy. But we must hold firm: only hymns to the gods and praises of virtuous men should be admitted into our State. If you allow the honeyed Muse to enter in epic or lyric form, then pleasure and pain -- not law and reason, which have always been deemed best -- will become the rulers of the State.
elegant: We may honor Homer's admirers and acknowledge him as the greatest poet, but only hymns to the gods and praises of famous men should enter the ideal State. Allow the honeyed Muse beyond this, and pleasure and pain will replace law and reason as rulers.
summary: Socrates issues the final verdict: Homer may be acknowledged as the greatest poet, but only hymns and praises of virtuous men may enter the ideal State. Allowing the "honeyed Muse" beyond this boundary would enthrone pleasure and pain over law and reason.
distilled: Homer is the greatest poet, but only hymns to gods and praises of the virtuous may be admitted -- the honeyed Muse would dethrone reason for pleasure and pain.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- "Educator of Hellas" (paideutes tes Hellados) was a conventional description of Homer -- "Honeyed Muse" (hedysmene Mousa) is a memorable phrase combining attraction and danger -- Only two genres permitted: hymns to gods and encomia of good men -- Cross-ref: Republic Book III (398a-b) where the same restriction was first proposed -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (377a-383c) for the earlier discussion of permissible content -- Cross-ref: Laws 801d-802e for the similar but somewhat relaxed position in Plato's later work

narrative:
"That is most true," he said.

"And now since we have reverted to the subject of poetry, let this our defence serve to show the reasonableness of our former judgment in sending away out of our State an art having the tendencies which we have described; for reason constrained us. But that she may not impute to us any harshness or want of politeness, let us tell her that there is an ancient quarrel between philosophy and poetry; of which there are many proofs, such as the saying of 'the yelping hound howling at her lord,' or of one 'mighty in the vain talk of fools,' and 'the mob of sages circumventing Zeus,' and the 'subtle thinkers who are beggars after all'; and there are innumerable other signs of ancient enmity between them. Notwithstanding this, let us assure our sweet friend and the sister arts of imitation that if she will only prove her title to exist in a well-ordered State we shall be delighted to receive her -- we are very conscious of her charms; but we may not on that account betray the truth. I dare say, Glaucon, that you are as much charmed by her as I am, especially when she appears in Homer?"

"Yes, indeed, I am greatly charmed."

"Shall I propose, then, that she be allowed to return from exile, but upon this condition only -- that she make a defence of herself in lyrical or some other metre?"
refashioned:
Glaucon: Most true.

Socrates: Since we've returned to the subject of poetry, let our argument serve as a defense of our earlier judgment in banishing this art from our State. Reason compelled us. But so that poetry cannot accuse us of harshness or discourtesy, let us acknowledge that there's an ancient quarrel between philosophy and poetry. There are many proofs of this: sayings like "the yelping hound howling at her lord," "mighty in the vain talk of fools," "the mob of sages circumventing Zeus," and "subtle thinkers who are beggars after all." The hostility runs deep.

Nevertheless, let us assure our sweet friend Poetry and her sister arts of imitation: if she can prove her right to exist in a well-ordered State, we'll gladly welcome her back. We're very conscious of her charms. But we cannot betray truth for the sake of charm. I suspect you're as enchanted by her as I am, Glaucon, especially in Homer?

Glaucon: Very much so.

Socrates: Then shall we propose that she be allowed to return from exile on one condition -- that she defend herself in verse or some other meter?
elegant: Reason compelled the banishment of imitative poetry. But there is an ancient quarrel between philosophy and poetry, and Socrates acknowledges poetry's charms even while insisting truth cannot be betrayed. He offers poetry a chance: if she can prove her right to exist in a well-ordered State, she'll be welcomed back. The condition is that she make her defense in verse.
summary: Socrates acknowledges the ancient quarrel between philosophy and poetry and confesses poetry's powerful charm. He offers poetry a conditional return from exile: if she can demonstrate her value to a well-ordered State, she will be gladly welcomed back.
distilled: Poetry may return from exile if she can prove her right to exist in a well-ordered State -- but truth must not be betrayed for charm.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The "ancient quarrel between philosophy and poetry" (palaia diaphora) is one of Plato's most famous formulations -- The quoted sayings are fragments from unknown poets attacking philosophers -- Poetry is personified as feminine throughout -- The offer of conditional return softens the banishment -- Cross-ref: Republic Books II-III for the original banishment -- Cross-ref: Ion 533d-534e on poetry's divine but irrational power -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 245a on divine madness including poetic inspiration -- Cross-ref: Laws 817a-d for Plato's later, somewhat gentler treatment of poetry

narrative: "Certainly. And we may further grant to those of her defenders who are lovers of poetry and yet not poets the permission to speak in prose on her behalf: let them show not only that she is pleasant but also useful to States and to human life, and we will listen in a kindly spirit; for if this can be proved we shall surely be the gainers -- I mean, if there is a use in poetry as well as a delight?"
refashioned: Socrates: And we can also grant her defenders -- those who love poetry but aren't poets themselves -- permission to argue on her behalf in prose. Let them show not only that poetry is pleasant but that she is genuinely useful to states and to human life. We'll listen with open minds, because if this can be proven, we'll be the ones who benefit. After all, wouldn't it be wonderful if poetry turned out to be both delightful and useful?
elegant: Poetry's non-poet defenders may also argue her case in prose. They must show she is not merely pleasant but genuinely useful to states and human life. If this can be proved, everyone gains.
summary: Socrates extends the invitation to poetry's prose defenders: they must demonstrate that poetry is not merely pleasurable but genuinely useful to political and human life. He expresses genuine hope that such a case can be made.
distilled: Poetry's defenders must prove she is not just pleasant but genuinely useful to human life and the State.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The invitation to prose defenders effectively invites literary criticism and philosophy of art -- The pleasure/utility distinction echoes Horace's later formulation (utile dulci) in Ars Poetica -- Socrates' expressed hope that the defense succeeds shows genuine ambivalence about banning poetry -- Cross-ref: Aristotle's Poetics can be read as the response to this invitation -- Cross-ref: Laws 817a-d for Plato's later treatment

narrative:
"Certainly," he said, "we shall be the gainers."

"If her defence fails, then, my dear friend, like other persons who are enamoured of something, but put a restraint upon themselves when they think their desires are opposed to their interests, so too must we after the manner of lovers give her up, though not without a struggle. We too are inspired by that love of poetry which the education of noble States has implanted in us, and therefore we would have her appear at her best and truest; but so long as she is unable to make good her defence, this argument of ours shall be a charm to us, which we will repeat to ourselves while we listen to her strains; that we may not fall away into the childish love of her which captivates the many. At all events we are well aware that poetry being such as we have described is not to be regarded seriously as attaining to the truth; and he who listens to her, fearing for the safety of the city which is within him, should be on his guard against her seductions and make our words his law."
refashioned:
Glaucon: We'd certainly be the gainers.

Socrates: But if her defense fails, then we must do what any person in love does when they realize their desire conflicts with their well-being -- we must give her up, though not without a struggle. We too feel the love of poetry that education in noble states has planted in us. We want her to appear at her best and truest. But as long as she can't make her case, our argument will serve as a protective charm that we repeat to ourselves while listening to her strains, so we don't fall into the childish infatuation that captivates the crowd.

We understand clearly that poetry as we've described it cannot be taken seriously as a path to truth. Anyone who listens to her should be vigilant about the safety of the city within his own soul, on guard against her seductions, making our words his law.
elegant: If poetry's defense fails, we must give her up like lovers who restrain themselves when desire opposes their interests -- reluctantly but firmly. Our argument becomes a protective charm we repeat while listening, lest we fall into childish infatuation. Poetry cannot be taken seriously as truth. The listener must guard the city within his soul against her seductions.
summary: If poetry cannot prove her worth, she must be given up like a lover whose desires conflict with one's good -- reluctantly but firmly. The philosophical argument serves as a protective charm against poetry's seductive power, guarding the internal city of the soul.
distilled: If poetry fails her defense, we must give her up like disciplined lovers, using philosophy as a charm against her seductions to protect the soul's inner city.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- "The city which is within him" (he entos polis) is a powerful expression of the Republic's central city-soul analogy -- The lover metaphor shows Plato's genuine ambivalence about banning poetry -- "Charm" (epode) -- argument as counter-spell against poetry's magic -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (376e-377a) on the education of the guardians through approved poetry -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 77e-78a on philosophy as a charm (epode) against the fear of death -- Cross-ref: Laws 659d-660a on using good poetry as a charm for the soul

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "I quite agree with you."

"Yes," I said, "my dear Glaucon, for great is the issue at stake, greater than appears, whether a man is to be good or bad. And what will any one be profited if under the influence of honour or money or power, aye, or under the excitement of poetry, he neglect justice and virtue?"
refashioned:
Glaucon: I completely agree.

Socrates: For the stakes are higher than they appear, Glaucon -- the question of whether a person becomes good or bad. What does anyone gain if, under the influence of honor, money, power, or even the excitement of poetry, he neglects justice and virtue?
elegant: The stakes are greater than they appear: whether a person becomes good or bad. No amount of honor, money, power, or poetic excitement profits anyone who neglects justice and virtue.
summary: Socrates emphasizes the ultimate stakes: the question of becoming good or bad. Nothing -- neither honor, money, power, nor the excitement of poetry -- profits anyone who neglects justice and virtue.
distilled: The real question is whether a person becomes good or bad, and nothing -- not even poetry -- is worth neglecting justice and virtue.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage transitions from the poetry critique to the rewards of justice -- Poetry is placed alongside honor, money, and power as potential distractions from virtue -- Echoes the language of Mark 8:36 ("what shall it profit a man") though preceding it by centuries -- Cross-ref: Republic Book I (352d-354a) on justice as the soul's proper virtue -- Cross-ref: Republic Book IX (580d-583a) on the philosopher's life as the happiest

narrative:
"Yes," he said; "I have been convinced by the argument, as I believe that any one else would have been."

"And yet no mention has been made of the greatest prizes and rewards which await virtue."

"What, are there any greater still? If there are, they must be of an inconceivable greatness."

"Why," I said, "what was ever great in a short time? The whole period of threescore years and ten is surely but a little thing in comparison with eternity?"

"Say rather 'nothing,'" he replied.

"And should an immortal being seriously think of this little space rather than of the whole?"

"Of the whole, certainly. But why do you ask?"

"Are you not aware," I said, "that the soul of man is immortal and imperishable?"

He looked at me in astonishment, and said: "No, by heaven: And are you really prepared to maintain this?"

"Yes," I said, "I ought to be, and you too -- there is no difficulty in proving it."

"I see a great difficulty; but I should like to hear you state this argument of which you make so light."

"Listen then."

"I am attending."

"There is a thing which you call good and another which you call evil?"

"Yes," he replied.

"Would you agree with me in thinking that the corrupting and destroying element is the evil, and the saving and improving element the good?"

"Yes."

"And you admit that every thing has a good and also an evil; as ophthalmia is the evil of the eyes and disease of the whole body; as mildew is of corn, and rot of timber, or rust of copper and iron: in everything, or in almost everything, there is an inherent evil and disease?"

"Yes," he said.

"And anything which is infected by any of these evils is made evil, and at last wholly dissolves and dies?"

"True."

"The vice and evil which is inherent in each is the destruction of each; and if this does not destroy them there is nothing else that will; for good certainly will not destroy them, nor again, that which is neither good nor evil."
refashioned:
Glaucon: I'm convinced by the argument, as anyone would be.

Socrates: Yet we haven't mentioned the greatest prizes and rewards that await virtue.

Glaucon: There are greater still? They must be inconceivably great.

Socrates: What was ever truly great in a short time? Our seventy years of life are nothing compared to eternity.

Glaucon: Not just little -- nothing at all.

Socrates: Should an immortal being seriously concern itself with this tiny span rather than the whole?

Glaucon: With the whole, certainly. But why do you ask?

Socrates: Are you not aware that the human soul is immortal and imperishable?

Glaucon looked at me in astonishment: No, by heaven! Are you seriously prepared to argue that?

Socrates: Yes, and so should you. It's not difficult to prove.

Glaucon: I see great difficulty. But I'd like to hear this argument you treat so lightly.

Socrates: Listen, then. You recognize something called good and something called evil?

Glaucon: Yes.

Socrates: The corrupting and destroying element is evil; the saving and improving element is good?

Glaucon: Yes.

Socrates: And everything has its own inherent evil: ophthalmia for the eyes, disease for the body, mildew for grain, rot for timber, rust for copper and iron. Almost everything carries within it its own specific disease.

Glaucon: Yes.

Socrates: Whatever is infected by its inherent evil is degraded and eventually destroyed entirely.

Glaucon: True.

Socrates: Only a thing's own inherent vice can destroy it. If that fails to destroy it, nothing else will -- certainly not good, nor anything neutral.
elegant: The greatest rewards of virtue are yet to come, and they concern eternity. Our seventy years are nothing beside it. The soul is immortal and imperishable. The proof: everything has its inherent evil (ophthalmia for eyes, rust for iron). Only a thing's own inherent vice can destroy it -- good cannot destroy, nor can anything neutral. If the inherent evil fails, nothing can.
summary: Socrates transitions to the soul's immortality. Everything has its own inherent evil that destroys it -- disease for the body, rust for metal, mildew for grain. Only this inherent vice can cause destruction; if it fails, nothing else can. This principle will prove the soul imperishable.
distilled: Only a thing's own inherent evil can destroy it -- if that fails, nothing else will, which points to the soul's immortality.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The transition from poetry critique to immortality is abrupt but deliberate: the rewards of justice require an afterlife -- Glaucon's astonishment at the immortality claim is dramatically effective -- "Threescore years and ten" (Psalm 90:10) -- a conventional lifespan -- The argument structure: each thing's specific evil is the only thing that can destroy it -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 72e-77d, 78b-84b, 102a-107b for the main arguments for immortality -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 245c-246a for the self-motion argument for immortality -- Cross-ref: Laws 904a-905d on divine justice and the afterlife

narrative:
"Certainly not."

"If, then, we find any nature which having this inherent corruption cannot be dissolved or destroyed, we may be certain that of such a nature there is no destruction?"

"That may be assumed."

"Well," I said, "and is there no evil which corrupts the soul?"

"Yes," he said, "there are all the evils which we were just now passing in review: unrighteousness, intemperance, cowardice, ignorance."
refashioned:
Glaucon: Certainly not.

Socrates: So if we find something whose inherent corruption cannot dissolve or destroy it, we can be certain that such a thing is indestructible?

Glaucon: That seems right.

Socrates: Now, is there an evil inherent to the soul?

Glaucon: Yes -- all the evils we just reviewed: injustice, intemperance, cowardice, and ignorance.
elegant: If something's inherent corruption cannot destroy it, then nothing can -- that thing is indestructible. The soul has inherent evils: injustice, intemperance, cowardice, and ignorance. The question becomes: do these destroy the soul?
summary: The argument advances: if something cannot be destroyed by its own inherent corruption, it is indestructible. The soul's inherent evils are injustice, intemperance, cowardice, and ignorance. The crucial question is whether these evils actually destroy the soul.
distilled: The soul has its own evils -- injustice, intemperance, cowardice, ignorance -- but the question is whether these actually destroy it.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The four soul-evils correspond to the four cardinal virtues (justice, temperance, courage, wisdom) discussed throughout the Republic -- The argument is moving toward showing that these evils degrade the soul but do not dissolve it -- Cross-ref: Republic Book IV (427e-434d) on the four virtues of the just city/soul -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 93a-95a for a different argument about the soul's relation to vice

narrative: "But does any of these dissolve or destroy her? -- and here do not let us fall into the error of supposing that the unjust and foolish man, when he is detected, perishes through his own injustice, which is an evil of the soul. Take the analogy of the body: The evil of the body is a disease which wastes and reduces and annihilates the body; and all the things of which we were just now speaking come to annihilation through their own corruption attaching to them and inhering in them and so destroying them. Is not this true?"
refashioned: Socrates: But do any of these evils actually dissolve or destroy the soul? Be careful here -- don't make the mistake of thinking that when an unjust or foolish person is caught and executed, he perishes because of his soul's own injustice. Consider the body's analogy: bodily disease wastes, reduces, and annihilates the body. Everything we discussed earlier is destroyed by its own inherent corruption attaching to it and consuming it from within. Isn't that right?
elegant: Do the soul's own evils dissolve or destroy it? Don't confuse execution of the unjust with the soul perishing from its own injustice. Bodily disease destroys the body from within -- that's how inherent corruption works. The question is whether injustice similarly dissolves the soul itself.
summary: Socrates asks whether the soul's own evils -- injustice, ignorance, cowardice -- actually dissolve the soul, as disease dissolves the body. He warns against confusing the external punishment of the unjust with internal destruction of the soul by its own vice.
distilled: The key question: does injustice dissolve the soul from within the way disease dissolves the body?
notes: Speakers: Socrates (with Glaucon assenting) -- The distinction between external punishment (execution) and internal destruction (the soul's evil consuming it) is crucial -- The body/soul analogy drives the argument: disease can kill the body, but can vice kill the soul? -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 91c-95a on whether the soul can be destroyed -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 477a-479e on vice as disease of the soul

narrative:
"Yes."

"Consider the soul in like manner. Does the injustice or other evil which exists in the soul waste and consume her? Do they by attaching to the soul and inhering in her at last bring her to death, and so separate her from the body?"

"Certainly not."

"And yet," I said, "it is unreasonable to suppose that anything can perish from without through affection of external evil which could not be destroyed from within by a corruption of its own?"
refashioned:
Socrates: Now consider the soul the same way. Does injustice or any other evil in the soul waste and consume her? Do these evils, by attaching to the soul and dwelling within her, eventually bring her to death and separate her from the body?

Glaucon: Certainly not.

Socrates: And yet it would be unreasonable to claim that something can be destroyed from outside by an external evil if it cannot be destroyed from within by its own inherent corruption.
elegant: Injustice and the soul's other evils do not waste and consume the soul the way disease consumes the body. They do not bring the soul to death. And if a thing's own inherent evil cannot destroy it, it would be unreasonable to suppose that any external evil could.
summary: The soul's inherent evils -- injustice, cowardice, ignorance -- do not consume and dissolve the soul the way disease dissolves the body. And if something cannot be destroyed by its own inherent corruption, nothing external can destroy it either.
distilled: The soul's own evils do not consume her, and what cannot be destroyed from within cannot be destroyed from without.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the crucial step in the immortality argument: the soul's inherent evils do not destroy the soul -- The principle that external evil cannot destroy what internal evil cannot is a logical extension of the earlier premise -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 106a-107a for the conclusion that what admits life (the soul) cannot admit death -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 245c-246a on the soul's self-moving nature as proof of immortality

narrative:
"It is," he replied.

"Consider," I said, "Glaucon, that even the badness of food, whether staleness, decomposition, or any other bad quality, when confined to the actual food, is not supposed to destroy the body; although, if the badness of food communicates corruption to the body, then we should say that the body has been destroyed by a corruption of itself, which is disease, brought on by this; but that the body, being one thing, can be destroyed by the badness of food, which is another, and which does not engender any natural infection -- this we shall absolutely deny?"

"Very true."

"And, on the same principle, unless some bodily evil can produce an evil of the soul, we must not suppose that the soul, which is one thing, can be dissolved by any merely external evil which belongs to another?"
refashioned:
Socrates: Consider this, Glaucon: bad food -- stale, decomposed, or otherwise tainted -- doesn't destroy the body directly. If bad food corrupts the body, we say the body was destroyed by its own disease, which the food happened to trigger. But the idea that the body, being one kind of thing, can be directly destroyed by the badness of food, which is a different kind of thing, without generating bodily disease -- we'd absolutely reject that.

Glaucon: Very true.

Socrates: By the same principle, unless some bodily evil can produce an evil within the soul itself, we cannot suppose that the soul can be dissolved by any merely external evil belonging to a different order of being.
elegant: Bad food doesn't directly destroy the body -- it triggers disease, and disease destroys the body. One kind of thing isn't directly destroyed by the evil of another kind. Similarly, unless bodily evil can generate soul-evil, the soul cannot be dissolved by any external evil belonging to a different order.
summary: Bad food triggers disease, which then destroys the body -- the food's badness doesn't directly destroy a different kind of thing. Likewise, unless bodily evil can generate evil within the soul, the soul cannot be dissolved by any external evil belonging to a different order of being.
distilled: Just as bad food can only destroy the body by triggering the body's own disease, external evils can only destroy the soul by generating the soul's own corruption -- which they cannot do.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The food analogy clarifies an important distinction: causation vs. direct destruction -- External evil must work through the thing's own inherent corruption to destroy it -- This principle blocks the objection that physical death or suffering could destroy the soul -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 64a-67b on the philosopher's indifference to bodily suffering -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 477a-479e on the distinction between bodily and psychic evils

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "there is reason in that."

"Either then, let us refute this conclusion, or, while it remains unrefuted, let us never say that fever, or any other disease, or the knife put to the throat, or even the cutting up of the whole body into the minutest pieces, can destroy the soul, until she herself is proved to become more unholy or unrighteous in consequence of these things being done to the body; but that the soul, or anything else if not destroyed by an internal evil, can be destroyed by an external one, is not to be affirmed by any man."

"And surely," he replied, "no one will ever prove that the souls of men become more unjust in consequence of death."
refashioned:
Glaucon: That's reasonable.

Socrates: Then either refute this argument, or accept the conclusion: we must never claim that fever, disease, a knife to the throat, or even chopping the body into the smallest possible pieces can destroy the soul -- unless someone proves that the soul becomes more unholy or unrighteous as a result of these physical injuries. That the soul, or anything, can be destroyed by an external evil when its own internal evil cannot destroy it -- no one should affirm this.

Glaucon: And surely no one will ever prove that people's souls become more unjust as a consequence of dying.
elegant: Unless physical destruction of the body makes the soul more unjust -- which no one can prove -- then fever, disease, violence, or even total dismemberment cannot destroy the soul. What cannot be destroyed by its own inherent evil cannot be destroyed by anything external.
summary: Neither disease, violence, nor even total bodily dismemberment can destroy the soul, unless someone proves that physical harm makes the soul more unjust. Since dying doesn't make souls more unrighteous, the soul is indestructible by external means.
distilled: Nothing physical -- not disease, violence, or death itself -- can destroy the soul, since bodily harm does not make the soul more unjust.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The graphic examples (knife, dismemberment) emphasize the argument's power -- Glaucon's point that death doesn't make souls more unjust is the clincher -- This argument differs from the Phaedo's arguments by focusing on the soul's relation to its own vices rather than its relation to Forms or self-motion -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 106a-107a (the final argument for immortality) -- Cross-ref: Apology 40c-41c on death as either sleep or a journey, neither of which is evil

narrative: "But if some one who would rather not admit the immortality of the soul boldly denies this, and says that the dying do really become more evil and unrighteous, then, if the speaker is right, I suppose that injustice, like disease, must be assumed to be fatal to the unjust, and that those who take this disorder die by the natural inherent power of destruction which evil has, and which kills them sooner or later, but in quite another way from that in which, at present, the wicked receive death at the hands of others as the penalty of their deeds?"
refashioned: Socrates: But suppose someone, determined to deny immortality, boldly claims that the dying really do become more evil and unjust. If that were true, then injustice would have to be fatal to the unjust, just as disease is fatal to the body. The unjust would die from the inherent destructive power of their own evil -- sooner or later, inevitably. This would be quite different from the current situation, where the wicked receive death as punishment from others for their crimes.
elegant: If dying made souls more unjust, then injustice would be fatal like disease -- the unjust would die from their own internal corruption, not from punishment by others. But this is clearly not what we observe.
summary: Socrates considers a counterargument: if dying really made souls more unjust, then injustice would have to be inherently fatal, killing the unjust from within like a disease -- quite different from the external punishment the wicked currently receive from others.
distilled: If injustice were truly fatal to the soul, the unjust would die from their own corruption rather than requiring external punishment.
notes: Speakers: Socrates (with Glaucon assenting) -- This is a reductio ad absurdum: if the opponent's position were true, injustice would be self-destructive, making punishment unnecessary -- But observation shows wicked people often thrive, suggesting injustice doesn't destroy the soul -- Cross-ref: Republic Book I (353e-354a) on injustice as the soul's disease -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 477a-479e on the analogy between bodily disease and soul-vice

narrative: "Nay," he said, "in that case injustice, if fatal to the unjust, will not be so very terrible to him, for he will be delivered from evil. But I rather suspect the opposite to be the truth, and that injustice which, if it have the power, will murder others, keeps the murderer alive -- aye, and well awake too; so far removed is her dwelling-place from being a house of death."
refashioned: Glaucon: Actually, if injustice were fatal to the unjust, it wouldn't be so terrible -- they'd be delivered from evil by their own death. But I suspect the truth is the opposite: injustice, which gives people the power to murder others, keeps the murderer himself very much alive -- wide awake, in fact. Injustice's dwelling place is the furthest thing from a house of death.
elegant: If injustice killed the unjust, it would almost be a mercy -- delivering them from evil. But the truth is the opposite: injustice murders others while keeping the murderer thriving and alert. Its dwelling is no house of death.
summary: Glaucon makes a sharp observation: if injustice were self-destructive, it would almost be merciful. But the truth is that injustice kills others while keeping the unjust person thriving -- further proof that the soul's own evil does not destroy it.
distilled: Injustice doesn't kill the unjust person -- it kills others while keeping the perpetrator alive and thriving.
notes: Speakers: Glaucon making an incisive philosophical point -- This is one of Glaucon's strongest contributions to the dialogue -- The observation that injustice empowers rather than destroys the unjust supports the immortality argument -- "So far removed is her dwelling-place from being a house of death" is a memorable formulation -- Cross-ref: Republic Book I (343b-344c) where Thrasymachus argued that injustice is profitable -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 469a-479e on whether it's worse to do or suffer injustice

narrative:
"True," I said; "if the inherent natural vice or evil of the soul is unable to kill or destroy her, hardly will that which is appointed to be the destruction of some other body, destroy a soul or anything else except that of which it was appointed to be the destruction."

"Yes, that can hardly be."

"But the soul which cannot be destroyed by an evil, whether inherent or external, must exist for ever, and if existing for ever, must be immortal?"

"Certainly."

"That is the conclusion," I said; "and, if a true conclusion, then the souls must always be the same, for if none be destroyed they will not diminish in number. Neither will they increase, for the increase of the immortal natures must come from something mortal, and all things would thus end in immortality."

"Very true."

"But this we cannot believe -- reason will not allow us -- any more than we can believe the soul, in her truest nature, to be full of variety and difference and dissimilarity."

"What do you mean?" he said.

"The soul," I said, "being, as is now proven, immortal, must be the fairest of compositions and cannot be compounded of many elements?"
refashioned:
Socrates: True. If the soul's own inherent evil cannot kill or destroy her, then certainly nothing appointed to destroy some other kind of body can destroy a soul. Evil destroys only what it's inherently appointed to destroy.

Glaucon: That's hard to deny.

Socrates: A soul that cannot be destroyed by any evil, whether inherent or external, must exist forever. And what exists forever is immortal.

Glaucon: Certainly.

Socrates: And if that's true, the total number of souls must remain constant. None are destroyed, so none diminish. And they cannot increase, because any increase in immortal beings would have to come from mortal origins, and eventually everything would become immortal.

Glaucon: Very true.

Socrates: But we can't believe that -- reason won't allow it. Nor can we believe the soul in its truest nature to be full of variety, difference, and internal conflict.

Glaucon: What do you mean?

Socrates: The soul, now proven immortal, must be the most unified of compositions. It cannot be made up of many elements.
elegant: If the soul's own vice cannot destroy her, nothing else can either. The soul is therefore immortal. The number of souls must be constant -- they neither diminish (none are destroyed) nor increase (immortal beings cannot arise from mortal ones). Furthermore, the immortal soul in its true nature must be simple and unified, not a compound of conflicting elements.
summary: Socrates concludes the immortality argument: since neither inherent nor external evil can destroy the soul, she is immortal. The number of souls must be constant. Moreover, the truly immortal soul must be simple and unified, not the complex, conflicted thing we experience in embodied life.
distilled: The soul, indestructible by any evil, is immortal; the number of souls is fixed; and the soul's true nature must be simple and unified.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The conclusion of the immortality argument from 10.34-10.42 -- The claim that the true soul is simple seems to contradict the tripartite soul of Book IV -- Plato may mean that the tripartite division belongs to the embodied soul, not the soul's essential nature -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 246a (where the soul's composite nature is affirmed) -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 78b-80d (argument that the simple/incomposite cannot be destroyed) -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 41a-42e on the creation and number of souls

narrative: "Certainly not. Her immortality is demonstrated by the previous argument, and there are many other proofs; but to see her as she really is, not as we now behold her, marred by communion with the body and other miseries, you must contemplate her with the eye of reason, in her original purity; and then her beauty will be revealed, and justice and injustice and all the things which we have described will be manifested more clearly. Thus far, we have spoken the truth concerning her as she appears at present, but we must remember also that we have seen her only in a condition which may be compared to that of the sea-god Glaucus, whose original image can hardly be discerned because his natural members are broken off and crushed and damaged by the waves in all sorts of ways, and incrustations have grown over them of seaweed and shells and stones, so that he is more like some monster than he is to his own natural form. And the soul which we behold is in a similar condition, disfigured by ten thousand ills. But not there, Glaucon, not there must we look."
refashioned:
Socrates: The soul's immortality has been demonstrated, and there are many other proofs. But to see the soul as she truly is -- not as we see her now, damaged by her partnership with the body and by the miseries of earthly life -- you must contemplate her with the eye of reason, in her original purity. Then her beauty will be revealed, and justice and injustice will be understood far more clearly.

So far we've described the soul as she appears in her present condition. But remember, we've seen her only in a state comparable to the sea-god Glaucus, whose original form can barely be recognized. His natural limbs have been broken, crushed, and battered by the waves. Incrustations of seaweed, shells, and stones have grown over him until he looks more like a monster than his true self. The soul we observe is in a similar condition -- disfigured by ten thousand afflictions.

But not there, Glaucon -- not there must we look.
elegant: The soul's immortality is proven, but to see her true nature -- not the version marred by bodily existence -- we must use the eye of reason. The embodied soul resembles the sea-god Glaucus, whose original beauty is unrecognizable beneath layers of seaweed, shells, and damage from the waves. We see a monster, not the god. The soul too is disfigured by ten thousand ills. We must look elsewhere for her true form.
summary: To see the soul's true nature, we must look beyond her embodied condition with the eye of reason. Like the sea-god Glaucus, encrusted with seaweed and battered beyond recognition by waves, the soul in the body is disfigured by countless afflictions and bears no resemblance to her original purity.
distilled: The embodied soul is like the sea-god Glaucus encrusted beyond recognition -- to see her true beauty, we must use the eye of reason.
notes: Speakers: Socrates (with Glaucon listening) -- Glaucus: a fisherman-turned-sea-god whose original form was obscured by marine growths (mentioned in various mythological sources) -- The pun on Glaucon/Glaucus is likely intentional -- This passage addresses the apparent contradiction between the soul's simplicity and Book IV's tripartite division -- The "eye of reason" echoes the Sun analogy from Book VI (508b-509a) -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 246a-249d on the soul's pre-embodied state -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 66b-67a on the body as obstacle to the soul's knowledge

narrative:
"Where then?"

"At her love of wisdom. Let us see whom she affects, and what society and converse she seeks in virtue of her near kindred with the immortal and eternal and divine; also how different she would become if wholly following this superior principle, and borne by a divine impulse out of the ocean in which she now is, and disengaged from the stones and shells and things of earth and rock which in wild variety spring up around her because she feeds upon earth, and is overgrown by the good things of this life as they are termed: then you would see her as she is, and know whether she has one shape only or many, or what her nature is. Of her affections and of the forms which she takes in this present life I think that we have now said enough."
refashioned:
Glaucon: Where should we look, then?

Socrates: At her love of wisdom. Observe what she reaches toward, what company she seeks, given her kinship with the immortal, eternal, and divine. Imagine how different she would become if she followed this higher principle entirely -- if a divine impulse carried her out of the ocean in which she's now submerged, and she was freed from the stones, shells, and earthly encrustations that have grown over her because she feeds on earthly things and is overgrown by what people call "the good things of this life." Then you would see her as she truly is and know whether she has one form or many, and what her nature really is.

But of her conditions and the shapes she takes in this present life, I think we've said enough.
elegant: Look at the soul's love of wisdom -- her kinship with the immortal and divine. Imagine her freed from the earthly encrustations that overgrow her -- the "good things" of this life that weigh her down. Liberated, borne by divine impulse out of the ocean, she would reveal her true nature: whether she is one or many, and what she really is.
summary: To see the soul's true nature, look at her love of wisdom and her kinship with the divine. If freed from earthly attachments -- the so-called good things of this life that overgrow her like barnacles -- she would reveal her genuine form.
distilled: The soul's true nature is revealed through her love of wisdom and kinship with the divine, visible only when freed from earthly encrustation.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- "Love of wisdom" (philosophia) -- the soul's deepest natural orientation -- The Glaucus metaphor is extended: the soul must be lifted from the ocean and cleaned of encrustation -- "Good things of this life" are ironically the things that obscure the soul's true nature -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 82d-83e on the philosopher's purification from bodily attachments -- Cross-ref: Republic Book VII (518b-519b) on turning the soul toward the light -- Cross-ref: Symposium 210a-212a on the ascent toward Beauty itself

narrative:
"True," he replied.

"And thus," I said, "we have fulfilled the conditions of the argument; we have not introduced the rewards and glories of justice, which, as you were saying, are to be found in Homer and Hesiod; but justice in her own nature has been shown to be best for the soul in her own nature. Let a man do what is just, whether he have the ring of Gyges or not, and even if in addition to the ring of Gyges he put on the helmet of Hades."
refashioned:
Glaucon: True.

Socrates: And so we've fulfilled the conditions of our argument. We haven't relied on the rewards and glories of justice as praised in Homer and Hesiod. Instead, we've shown that justice in its own nature is best for the soul in its own nature. A person should act justly whether or not he possesses the Ring of Gyges -- even if he added the Helmet of Hades to the ring.
elegant: The argument is complete without appeal to external rewards. Justice in its own nature has been shown best for the soul in its own nature. Act justly with or without the Ring of Gyges -- even with the Helmet of Hades added.
summary: Socrates declares the core argument fulfilled: justice has been shown to be inherently good for the soul, without relying on external rewards. One should be just even with perfect invisibility -- the Ring of Gyges and the Helmet of Hades combined.
distilled: Justice is best for the soul in its own nature -- be just even if you possess the Ring of Gyges and the Helmet of Hades.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Ring of Gyges: from Book II (359c-360d), Glaucon's challenge -- a ring granting invisibility, testing whether anyone would remain just without consequences -- Helmet of Hades (cap of invisibility): from Homer, Iliad V.844-845, worn by Athena -- Homer and Hesiod: the two great epic poets of Greek tradition -- This marks the fulfillment of the challenge posed in Book II: to prove justice is good in itself, not merely for its rewards -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (357a-367e) for Glaucon's and Adeimantus's original challenge -- Cross-ref: Republic Book IX (580a-588a) for the three proofs that the just life is happiest

narrative:
"Very true."

"And now, Glaucon, there will be no harm in further enumerating how many and how great are the rewards which justice and the other virtues procure to the soul from gods and men, both in life and after death."

"Certainly not," he said.

"Will you repay me, then, what you borrowed in the argument?"

"What did I borrow?"

"The assumption that the just man should appear unjust and the unjust just: for you were of opinion that even if the true state of the case could not possibly escape the eyes of gods and men, still this admission ought to be made for the sake of the argument, in order that pure justice might be weighed against pure injustice. Do you remember?"
refashioned:
Glaucon: Very true.

Socrates: Now, Glaucon, there's no harm in also counting up the many great rewards that justice and the other virtues bring from gods and men, both during life and after death.

Glaucon: Certainly not.

Socrates: Then will you repay what you borrowed in the argument?

Glaucon: What did I borrow?

Socrates: The assumption that the just man should appear unjust and the unjust man appear just. You argued that even if the truth couldn't really escape the gods' notice, this concession should be made for argument's sake, so that pure justice could be weighed against pure injustice. Do you remember?
elegant: Having proven justice's intrinsic worth, Socrates now adds its external rewards from gods and men. He asks Glaucon to repay what was "borrowed" in Book II: the assumption that the just man appears unjust and vice versa, made so pure justice could be weighed against pure injustice without external considerations.
summary: With justice's intrinsic value established, Socrates turns to its external rewards. He asks Glaucon to return the concession from Book II: that the just man would appear unjust, stripped of all reputation, so justice could be evaluated purely on its own merits.
distilled: Having proved justice intrinsically best, Socrates now reclaims the concession from Book II and adds justice's external rewards from gods and men.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage formally marks the transition from intrinsic to extrinsic rewards of justice -- The "borrowing" metaphor for the Book II assumption is elegant and precise -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (360e-362c) where Glaucon described the just man stripped of reputation and the unjust man with a perfect reputation -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (367b-368a) where Adeimantus demanded that justice be praised for itself, not its rewards

narrative:
"I should be much to blame if I had forgotten."

"Then, as the cause is decided, I demand on behalf of justice that the estimation in which she is held by gods and men and which we acknowledge to be her due should now be restored to her by us; since she has been shown to confer reality, and not to deceive those who truly possess her, let what has been taken from her be given back, that so she may win that palm of appearance which is hers also, and which she gives to her own."
refashioned:
Glaucon: I'd be very much at fault if I'd forgotten.

Socrates: Then since the case is decided, I demand on behalf of justice that the reputation she deserves from gods and men be fully restored. She has been shown to confer genuine benefit, not to deceive those who truly possess her. Let what was stripped away for the argument be given back, so justice may also win the prize of appearance that rightfully belongs to her -- the good reputation she naturally grants to those who practice her.
elegant: The case is decided. Justice deserves her full reputation restored. She confers reality, not deception, on those who possess her. Now let justice also claim the prize of appearance -- the good reputation she naturally grants her practitioners.
summary: With justice's intrinsic worth proven, Socrates demands that her reputation be restored. Justice genuinely benefits those who possess her and deserves to be recognized -- both for her inner reality and for the outward reputation she naturally bestows.
distilled: Justice, proven genuinely beneficial, now deserves her full reputation restored -- both the reality and the appearance of goodness.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- "Palm of appearance" echoes athletic competition metaphors -- Justice is personified throughout as a female figure deserving vindication -- The restoration of reputation reverses the stripping done in Book II -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (361b-362a) where the just man's reputation was entirely removed -- Cross-ref: Republic Book IX (580b-583a) where justice was shown to produce the happiest life

narrative:
"The demand," he said, "is just."

"In the first place," I said -- "and this is the first thing which you will have to give back -- the nature both of the just and unjust is truly known to the gods."

"Granted."

"And if they are both known to them, one must be the friend and the other the enemy of the gods, as we admitted from the beginning?"

"True."

"And the friend of the gods may be supposed to receive from them all things at their best, excepting only such evil as is the necessary consequence of former sins?"

"Certainly."

"Then this must be our notion of the just man, that even when he is in poverty or sickness, or any other seeming misfortune, all things will in the end work together for good to him in life and death: for the gods have a care of any one whose desire is to become just and to be like God, as far as man can attain the divine likeness, by the pursuit of virtue?"

"Yes," he said; "if he is like God he will surely not be neglected by him."

"And of the unjust may not the opposite be supposed?"

"Certainly."

"Such, then, are the palms of victory which the gods give the just?"
refashioned:
Glaucon: The demand is just.

Socrates: First -- and this is what you must return -- the gods truly know the nature of both the just and the unjust.

Glaucon: Granted.

Socrates: And since both are known to them, one must be their friend and the other their enemy, as we agreed at the beginning?

Glaucon: True.

Socrates: The friend of the gods may be expected to receive from them everything at its best, except for whatever suffering is the necessary consequence of past wrongs?

Glaucon: Certainly.

Socrates: Then we must hold that even when the just person suffers poverty, illness, or any other seeming misfortune, all things will ultimately work together for his good, in life and in death. The gods care for anyone whose desire is to become just and to be like God, as far as human nature can approach the divine through the pursuit of virtue.

Glaucon: Yes -- if he resembles God, he surely won't be neglected by God.

Socrates: And the opposite for the unjust?

Glaucon: Certainly.

Socrates: These, then, are the prizes the gods award to the just.
elegant: The gods know the just from the unjust. The just person is God's friend and receives all things for the best, even if he suffers apparent misfortune -- all things ultimately work for his good, in life and death. The gods care for anyone who pursues virtue and seeks to become like God. The unjust receive the opposite.
summary: Since the gods know the just from the unjust, the just person is God's friend and all things ultimately work for his good, even apparent misfortunes. The gods care for anyone who seeks to become like God through virtue. The unjust receive the opposite fate.
distilled: The gods know and reward the just -- all things work together for his good -- while the unjust receive the opposite.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- "All things will work together for good" (panta eis agathon teleutai) anticipates Romans 8:28 by several centuries -- "To be like God" (homoiosis theo) becomes a central concept in later Platonism -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (362d-367e) for the original concession now being restored -- Cross-ref: Theaetetus 176b on becoming like God as the aim of philosophy -- Cross-ref: Laws 716c-d on the virtuous person as dear to God -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 90a-d on becoming like the divine

narrative:
"That is my conviction."

"And what do they receive of men? Look at things as they really are, and you will see that the clever unjust are in the case of runners, who run well from the starting-place to the goal but not back again from the goal: they go off at a great pace, but in the end only look foolish, slinking away with their ears draggling on their shoulders, and without a crown; but the true runner comes to the finish and receives the prize and is crowned. And this is the way with the just; he who endures to the end of every action and occasion of his entire life has a good report and carries off the prize which men have to bestow."
refashioned:
Glaucon: That's my conviction too.

Socrates: And what do the just receive from other people? Look at how things really play out. The clever unjust are like runners who sprint impressively from start to finish but can't make it back -- they start fast but end up slinking away looking foolish, ears drooping, with no crown. The true runner finishes the race and receives the prize. So it is with the just: the person who endures to the end in every action and situation of his entire life earns a good reputation and wins the prizes that humanity has to offer.
elegant: The clever unjust are like runners who sprint well but can't finish -- they end up slinking away crownless. The just person, who endures to the end of his whole life, finishes the race and wins the prize from both gods and men.
summary: Using the runner analogy, Socrates shows that clever unjust people start strong but finish poorly, while the just person who endures to the end of life wins the full prize of human reputation and honor.
distilled: The unjust sprint well but can't finish the race; the just person endures to the end and wins the crown.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The athletic metaphor (diaulos race -- there and back) was central to Greek culture -- "Ears draggling on shoulders" is a vivid image of defeated runners -- This counters the Book II claim that the unjust person would always enjoy a good reputation -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (360e-362c) where the unjust man was described as having a perfect reputation throughout life -- Cross-ref: 2 Timothy 4:7-8 ("I have finished the race") for a later echo of this metaphor

narrative:
"True."

"And now you must allow me to repeat of the just the blessings which you were attributing to the fortunate unjust. I shall say of them, what you were saying of the others, that as they grow older, they become rulers in their own city if they care to be; they marry whom they like and give in marriage to whom they will; all that you said of the others I now say of these. And, on the other hand, of the unjust I say that the greater number, even though they escape in their youth, are found out at last and look foolish at the end of their course, and when they come to be old and miserable are flouted alike by stranger and citizen; they are beaten and then come those things unfit for ears polite, as you truly term them; they will be racked and have their eyes burned out, as you were saying. And you may suppose that I have repeated the remainder of your tale of horrors. But will you let me assume, without reciting them, that these things are true?"
refashioned:
Socrates: Now let me attribute to the just all the blessings you once attributed to the fortunate unjust. As they grow older, the just become rulers in their city if they choose. They marry whom they like and give their children in marriage to whomever they wish. Everything you said about the unjust, I now say about the just.

And of the unjust, I say the reverse: most of them, even if they escape detection in their youth, are found out in the end and look foolish at the close of their lives. When they're old and miserable, they're scorned by strangers and citizens alike. They're beaten, and then come the things you aptly called "unfit for polite ears" -- they'll be racked and have their eyes burned out, as you described. You can assume I've repeated all the rest of your catalogue of horrors. Will you let me take it as established that these things are true?
elegant: The blessings once attributed to the fortunate unjust now belong to the just: political power, favorable marriages, social standing. The unjust, mostly caught in the end, face old age in misery -- scorned, beaten, and subjected to the very tortures once reserved for the just in Glaucon's Book II scenario.
summary: Socrates reverses the Book II scenario: the just person now enjoys all the blessings previously attributed to the successful unjust -- power, marriage, respect. The unjust, mostly found out eventually, face misery, scorn, and the very punishments Glaucon once described for the just.
distilled: The rewards Glaucon once gave the unjust now belong to the just, and the punishments he assigned the just now await the unjust.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This directly reverses Glaucon's descriptions from Book II (361b-362a) -- "Things unfit for ears polite" echoes Glaucon's own language from his earlier speech -- The reversal completes the argument's symmetry: Book II stripped justice of rewards; Book X restores them -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (360e-362a) for Glaucon's original description of the unjust man's rewards and the just man's sufferings

narrative:
"Certainly," he said, "what you say is true."

"These, then, are the prizes and rewards and gifts which are bestowed upon the just by gods and men in this present life, in addition to the other good things which justice of herself provides."

"Yes," he said; "and they are fair and lasting."

"And yet," I said, "all these are as nothing, either in number or greatness in comparison with those other recompenses which await both just and unjust after death. And you ought to hear them, and then both just and unjust will have received from us a full payment of the debt which the argument owes to them."

"Speak," he said; "there are few things which I would more gladly hear."
refashioned:
Glaucon: What you say is true.

Socrates: These, then, are the prizes, rewards, and gifts that gods and men bestow on the just during this life, in addition to the good things justice provides by her own nature.

Glaucon: Yes, and they are fine and lasting.

Socrates: Yet all of these are nothing -- in number or in greatness -- compared to the rewards that await both the just and unjust after death. You need to hear about those, and then our argument will have fully paid its debt to both the just and the unjust.

Glaucon: Tell me -- there are few things I'd rather hear.
elegant: The earthly rewards of justice from gods and men, fine as they are, are nothing compared to what awaits the just and unjust after death. This final account will complete the argument's full payment of its debt.
summary: Socrates declares that the earthly rewards of justice, however fine, are nothing compared to the afterlife rewards. He prepares to tell the Myth of Er, which will show the full consequences awaiting both the just and unjust after death.
distilled: All earthly rewards of justice are nothing compared to what awaits after death -- the argument's final and greatest debt remains to be paid.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the transition to the Myth of Er, the dialogue's closing myth -- "Full payment of the debt" frames the dialogue as an obligation to demonstrate justice's total worth -- Glaucon's eagerness sets up the dramatic conclusion -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 523a-527a for the eschatological myth in that dialogue -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 107d-114c for the myth of the afterlife in that dialogue -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 246a-249d for the cosmic myth of the soul

narrative:
"Well," I said, "I will tell you a tale; not one of the tales which Odysseus tells to the hero Alcinous, yet this too is a tale of a hero, Er the son of Armenius, a Pamphylian by birth. He was slain in battle, and ten days afterwards, when the bodies of the dead were taken up already in a state of corruption, his body was found unaffected by decay, and carried away home to be buried. And on the twelfth day, as he was lying on the funeral pile, he returned to life and told them what he had seen in the other world.

He said that when his soul left the body he went on a journey with a great company, and that they came to a mysterious place at which there were two openings in the earth; they were near together, and over against them were two other openings in the heaven above. In the intermediate space there were judges seated, who commanded the just, after they had given judgment on them and had bound their sentences in front of them, to ascend by the heavenly way on the right hand; and in like manner the unjust were bidden by them to descend by the lower way on the left hand; these also bore the symbols of their deeds, but fastened on their backs.

He drew near, and they told him that he was to be the messenger who would carry the report of the other world to men, and they bade him hear and see all that was to be heard and seen in that place. Then he beheld and saw on one side the souls departing at either opening of heaven and earth when sentence had been given on them; and at the two other openings other souls, some ascending out of the earth dusty and worn with travel, some descending out of heaven clean and bright. And arriving ever and anon they seemed to have come from a long journey, and they went forth with gladness into the meadow, where they encamped as at a festival; and those who knew one another embraced and conversed, the souls which came from earth curiously enquiring about the things above, and the souls which came from heaven about the things beneath."
refashioned:
Socrates: I will tell you a tale -- not one of those tales Odysseus told to King Alcinous, but a tale of a hero nonetheless. Er, son of Armenius, was a Pamphylian who was killed in battle. Ten days later, when the other bodies were already decomposing, his was found perfectly preserved. He was carried home for burial, and on the twelfth day, lying on the funeral pyre, he came back to life and described what he had seen in the world beyond.

He said that after his soul left his body, he traveled with a great company of souls to a mysterious place. There he found two openings in the earth close together, and directly above them two openings in the sky. Between them sat judges who examined each soul and pronounced sentence: the just were directed to ascend through the right-hand heavenly opening, wearing their judgments displayed on their chests; the unjust were sent downward through the left-hand earthly opening, with their judgments fastened to their backs.

When Er approached, the judges told him he was to be the messenger who would report on the other world to the living. They instructed him to observe everything carefully. He watched as judged souls departed through the openings of heaven and earth, while from the other two openings souls arrived -- some rising from the earth, dusty and travel-worn, others descending from heaven, clean and radiant. They seemed to have come from long journeys, and they gathered with joy in the meadow between, camping as if at a festival. Those who recognized each other embraced and talked, the souls from below asking eagerly about heaven, and those from above about the underworld.
elegant: Socrates tells the Myth of Er: a Pamphylian soldier killed in battle whose body didn't decay and who revived on the funeral pyre to report what he'd seen. His soul traveled to a place of judgment with four openings -- two in earth, two in heaven. Judges sent the just upward to the right and the unjust downward to the left. Er was appointed messenger. He watched souls departing after judgment and arriving from completed journeys -- dusty pilgrims from below, radiant travelers from above -- gathering joyfully in a meadow to share stories.
summary: Socrates begins the Myth of Er: a soldier who died in battle, remained uncorrupted, and revived to tell of the afterlife. His soul traveled to a place of judgment with four openings, where the just ascended to heaven and the unjust descended underground, while returning souls gathered in a meadow to share their experiences.
distilled: Er, a soldier who returned from death, describes a cosmic judgment place where the just ascend to heaven and the unjust descend to the underworld.
notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- Er (Heros) the son of Armenius, a Pamphylian: possibly symbolic ("Er" may relate to "Eros"; Pamphylia means "land of all tribes") -- Alcinous: king of the Phaeacians in the Odyssey, to whom Odysseus tells his tale of the underworld (Odyssey Books 9-12, especially the Nekyia in Book 11) -- The four-opening cosmology (two earth, two heaven) is unique to this myth -- The meadow gathering echoes Odyssey 11 (the Nekyia) -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 523a-527a for a different Platonic afterlife myth -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 107d-114c for the Phaedo's underworld geography -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 248c-249b on the cycle of reincarnation

narrative:
"And they told one another of what had happened by the way, those from below weeping and sorrowing at the remembrance of the things which they had endured and seen in their journey beneath the earth (now the journey lasted a thousand years), while those from above were describing heavenly delights and visions of inconceivable beauty.

The Story, Glaucon, would take too long to tell; but the sum was this: -- He said that for every wrong which they had done to any one they suffered tenfold; or once in a hundred years -- such being reckoned to be the length of man's life, and the penalty being thus paid ten times in a thousand years. If, for example, there were any who had been the cause of many deaths, or had betrayed or enslaved cities or armies, or been guilty of any other evil behaviour, for each and all of their offences they received punishment ten times over, and the rewards of beneficence and justice and holiness were in the same proportion.

I need hardly repeat what he said concerning young children dying almost as soon as they were born. Of piety and impiety to gods and parents, and of murderers, there were retributions other and greater far which he described.

He mentioned that he was present when one of the spirits asked another, 'Where is Ardiaeus the Great?' (Now this Ardiaeus lived a thousand years before the time of Er: he had been the tyrant of some city of Pamphylia, and had murdered his aged father and his elder brother, and was said to have committed many other abominable crimes.)

The answer of the other spirit was: 'He comes not hither and will never come. And this,' said he, 'was one of the dreadful sights which we ourselves witnessed. We were at the mouth of the cavern, and, having completed all our experiences, were about to reascend, when of a sudden Ardiaeus appeared and several others, most of whom were tyrants; and there were also besides the tyrants private individuals who had been great criminals: they were just, as they fancied, about to return into the upper world, but the mouth, instead of admitting them, gave a roar, whenever any of these incurable sinners or some one who had not been sufficiently punished tried to ascend; and then wild men of fiery aspect, who were standing by and heard the sound, seized and carried them off; and Ardiaeus and others they bound head and foot and hand, and threw them down and flayed them with scourges, and dragged them along the road at the side, carding them on thorns like wool, and declaring to the passers-by what were their crimes, and that they were being taken away to be cast into hell.'

And of all the many terrors which they had endured, he said that there was none like the terror which each of them felt at that moment, lest they should hear the voice; and when there was silence, one by one they ascended with exceeding joy. These, said Er, were the penalties and retributions, and there were blessings as great."
refashioned:
Socrates: The souls shared their stories. Those from below wept and grieved, remembering what they had endured during their thousand-year journey underground. Those from above described heavenly delights and visions of inconceivable beauty.

The full story, Glaucon, would take too long, but here's the essence: for every wrong committed, the punishment was tenfold. A human life was reckoned at a hundred years, so the penalty was paid ten times over across a thousand years. Those who caused many deaths, betrayed cities, enslaved armies, or committed other grave evils received punishment tenfold for each offense. The rewards for beneficence, justice, and holiness were proportional.

Regarding infants who died at birth, and regarding crimes of impiety toward gods and parents and murder, there were punishments far greater still.

Er reported being present when one spirit asked another: "Where is Ardiaeus the Great?" Ardiaeus had been a tyrant in Pamphylia a thousand years earlier -- he had murdered his elderly father and elder brother and committed many other horrible crimes.

The answer came: "He does not come here and never will." The spirit continued: "This was one of the most terrifying sights we witnessed. We were at the mouth of the cavern, ready to reascend, when suddenly Ardiaeus appeared along with several others -- mostly tyrants, plus some private citizens who had been great criminals. They believed they were about to return to the upper world. But the mouth of the cavern gave a great roar whenever any incurable sinner or insufficiently punished soul tried to ascend. Then wild, fiery-looking men who stood by, hearing the roar, seized them and carried them off. They bound Ardiaeus and the others hand and foot, threw them down, flayed them with whips, and dragged them along the roadside, tearing them on thorns like wool being carded, announcing their crimes to everyone passing by: they were being taken to be cast into Tartarus."

Er said that of all the terrors endured in that place, nothing compared to the dread each soul felt at that moment -- the fear of hearing the roar. When silence came, each ascended with overwhelming joy. Such were the punishments and retributions; the blessings were equally great.
elegant: Souls from below wept remembering their thousand-year punishments; souls from above described heavenly beauty. Every wrong was repaid tenfold across a thousand years, and rewards for virtue were proportional. The tyrant Ardiaeus, who murdered his father and brother, was denied ascent permanently -- when he and other incurable sinners tried to leave, the cavern roared and fiery men seized them, bound them, flayed them on thorns, and dragged them toward Tartarus. Of all terrors, the worst was the dread of hearing that roar. When silence came, the eligible souls ascended with overwhelming joy.
summary: Er describes the afterlife's system of justice: every wrong is repaid tenfold over a thousand years, with proportional rewards for virtue. The tyrant Ardiaeus, a patricide, is permanently denied return. Incurable sinners who attempt to ascend are seized by fiery beings, tortured, and cast into Tartarus -- a terror surpassing all others.
distilled: Every wrong is repaid tenfold over a thousand years, and incurable sinners like the tyrant Ardiaeus are permanently cast into Tartarus.
notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- Ardiaeus the Great: a Pamphylian tyrant, patricide and fratricide -- his permanent damnation represents the worst category of sinner -- Tartarus: the deepest region of the underworld, reserved for the worst offenders -- The tenfold punishment over 1000 years establishes proportional cosmic justice -- "Carding them on thorns like wool" is a memorably horrific image -- The roar of the cavern as a mechanism of judgment is unique to this myth -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 525c-526d on incurable sinners permanently punished in Tartarus -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 113d-114b on Tartarus and different levels of post-mortem punishment -- Cross-ref: Laws 904d-905b on proportional divine justice
