narrative: "Now when the spirits which were in the meadow had tarried seven days, on the eighth they were obliged to proceed on their journey, and, on the fourth day after, he said that they came to a place where they could see from above a line of light, straight as a column, extending right through the whole heaven and through the earth, in colour resembling the rainbow, only brighter and purer; another day's journey brought them to the place, and there, in the midst of the light, they saw the ends of the chains of heaven let down from above: for this light is the belt of heaven, and holds together the circle of the universe, like the under-girders of a trireme. From these ends is extended the spindle of Necessity, on which all the revolutions turn. The shaft and hook of this spindle are made of steel, and the whorl is made partly of steel and also partly of other materials."
refashioned:
Socrates: After the spirits had rested in the meadow for seven days, on the eighth they were required to continue their journey. Four days later, they came to a vantage point where they could see a column of light stretching straight through all of heaven and earth -- like a rainbow but far brighter and purer. After another day's travel they reached this place and saw, in the midst of the light, the ends of heaven's chains suspended from above. This light is the belt of heaven -- it holds the universe together like the undergirding ropes of a warship.

From these chain-ends extends the Spindle of Necessity, on which all cosmic revolutions turn. The shaft and hook of this spindle are made of adamant, and the whorl is made partly of adamant and partly of other materials.
elegant: After seven days in the meadow, the spirits journeyed for four more days to a place where a column of brilliant light, brighter than a rainbow, extended through all heaven and earth. This light is the belt holding the universe together, like a trireme's undergirding. From it extends the Spindle of Necessity, on which all cosmic revolutions turn.
summary: The spirits journey to the cosmic axis: a column of light stretching through heaven and earth, brighter than a rainbow, which holds the universe together. From it extends the Spindle of Necessity, the cosmic mechanism on which all celestial revolutions turn.
distilled: At the cosmic center, a pillar of light holds the universe together, and from it extends the Spindle of Necessity governing all celestial motion.
notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- The Spindle of Necessity (Ananke) is a cosmological model of the universe -- The light column may represent the Milky Way or the cosmic axis -- Trireme undergirding (hypozomata): ropes binding the hull of a Greek warship -- The cosmology presented here is geocentric, with nested spheres -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 34b-40d for Plato's more detailed cosmological account -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 108c-113c for a different cosmological picture -- Cross-ref: Laws 898d-899b on the cosmic soul governing celestial motion

narrative: "Now the whorl is in form like the whorl used on earth; and the description of it implied that there is one large hollow whorl which is quite scooped out, and into this is fitted another lesser one, and another, and another, and four others, making eight in all, like vessels which fit into one another; the whorls show their edges on the upper side, and on their lower side all together form one continuous whorl. This is pierced by the spindle, which is driven home through the centre of the eighth. The first and outermost whorl has the rim broadest, and the seven inner whorls are narrower, in the following proportions -- the sixth is next to the first in size, the fourth next to the sixth; then comes the eighth; the seventh is fifth, the fifth is sixth, the third is seventh, last and eighth comes the second. The largest (of fixed stars) is spangled, and the seventh (or sun) is brightest; the eighth (or moon) coloured by the reflected light of the seventh; the second and fifth (Saturn and Mercury) are in colour like one another, and yellower than the preceding; the third (Venus) has the whitest light; the fourth (Mars) is reddish; the sixth (Jupiter) is in whiteness second."
refashioned:
Socrates: The whorl resembles an earthly spindle whorl in shape. Imagine one large hollow whorl, completely hollowed out, with a smaller one fitted inside it, then another inside that, and so on -- eight in all, nested like bowls stacked within each other. Their rims show at the top, while below they form a single continuous surface. The spindle passes through the center of all eight.

The outermost whorl has the broadest rim. The inner seven vary in width: the sixth is next largest after the first, then the fourth, then the eighth, then the seventh, then the fifth, then the third, and the second is narrowest. The outermost (the sphere of fixed stars) is spangled with stars. The seventh (the Sun) is the brightest. The eighth (the Moon) gets its light from the seventh. The second (Saturn) and fifth (Mercury) are similar in color -- yellower than the others. The third (Venus) has the whitest light. The fourth (Mars) is reddish. The sixth (Jupiter) is second in whiteness.
elegant: Eight nested whorls, like bowls within bowls, form the cosmic spindle. The outermost sphere of fixed stars is spangled; the Sun is brightest; the Moon reflects the Sun's light; Saturn and Mercury are yellow; Venus is whitest; Mars is reddish; Jupiter is second in whiteness. Each planet's position and color correspond to its whorl.
summary: Socrates describes the cosmic structure as eight nested whorls spinning on the Spindle of Necessity, each representing a celestial sphere. He details their relative sizes and colors: the fixed stars are spangled, the Sun brightest, the Moon reflective, and each planet has its characteristic color.
distilled: The universe consists of eight nested celestial spheres spinning on the Spindle of Necessity, each with its own characteristic size and color.
notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- The eight whorls represent: (1) fixed stars, (2) Saturn, (3) Venus, (4) Mars, (5) Mercury, (6) Jupiter, (7) Sun, (8) Moon -- This is a geocentric model with nested spheres -- The descriptions of planetary colors match ancient astronomical observation -- The Moon's reflected light from the Sun is scientifically correct -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 38c-39e for a more detailed account of planetary arrangement -- Cross-ref: Laws 821b-822d on the true motions of celestial bodies -- The mathematical proportions of rim widths may reflect Pythagorean cosmology

narrative:
"Now the whole spindle has the same motion; but, as the whole revolves in one direction, the seven inner circles move slowly in the other, and of these the swiftest is the eighth; next in swiftness are the seventh, sixth, and fifth, which move together; third in swiftness appeared to move according to the law of this reversed motion the fourth; the third appeared fourth and the second fifth.

The spindle turns on the knees of Necessity; and on the upper surface of each circle is a siren, who goes round with them, hymning a single tone or note. The eight together form one harmony; and round about, at equal intervals, there is another band, three in number, each sitting upon her throne: these are the Fates, daughters of Necessity, who are clothed in white robes and have chaplets upon their heads, Lachesis and Clotho and Atropos, who accompany with their voices the harmony of the sirens -- Lachesis singing of the past, Clotho of the present, Atropos of the future; Clotho from time to time assisting with a touch of her right hand the revolution of the outer circle of the whorl or spindle, and Atropos with her left hand touching and guiding the inner ones, and Lachesis laying hold of either in turn, first with one hand and then with the other."
refashioned:
Socrates: The whole spindle revolves together, but within that single motion the seven inner spheres rotate slowly in the opposite direction. Among these, the fastest is the eighth (the Moon); next in speed are the seventh (Sun), sixth (Jupiter), and fifth (Mercury), which move together; the fourth (Mars) appeared to move third-fastest in this reversed rotation; the third (Venus) appeared fourth in speed, and the second (Saturn) fifth.

The spindle turns on the knees of Necessity herself. On the upper surface of each sphere sits a Siren, revolving with it, each singing a single continuous note. The eight notes together create one harmony.

Around them, seated at equal intervals on thrones, are three figures: the Fates, daughters of Necessity, robed in white with garlands on their heads -- Lachesis, Clotho, and Atropos. Their voices accompany the Sirens' harmony: Lachesis sings of the past, Clotho of the present, Atropos of the future. Clotho periodically assists the outermost sphere's revolution with a touch of her right hand; Atropos guides the inner spheres with her left; and Lachesis touches both in turn, alternating hands.
elegant: The spindle revolves as one, while the inner spheres counter-rotate at different speeds. Eight Sirens, one per sphere, each sing a single note that together form one cosmic harmony. Three Fates, daughters of Necessity, preside: Lachesis (the past), Clotho (the present), and Atropos (the future) -- their voices accompanying the music as their hands guide the revolution of the spheres.
summary: The cosmic spindle's spheres counter-rotate at different speeds. Eight Sirens produce one harmony, each singing a single note. The three Fates -- Lachesis (past), Clotho (present), and Atropos (future) -- daughters of Necessity, sing along and guide the revolution of the spheres with their hands.
distilled: Eight Sirens create cosmic harmony while the three Fates -- past, present, future -- guide the universe's revolutions on the Spindle of Necessity.
notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- The Sirens here are benevolent cosmic musicians, not the dangerous Homeric Sirens (Odyssey XII) -- The "Harmony of the Spheres" (music of the spheres) influenced Pythagorean and later Western cosmological thought -- The three Fates (Moirai): Lachesis ("allotter"), Clotho ("spinner"), Atropos ("unturnable/inevitable") -- Necessity (Ananke) is the supreme cosmic principle in this myth -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 35b-36d on the World Soul's composition and harmony -- Cross-ref: Cratylus 403b on the Fates -- Cross-ref: Laws 960c on cosmic order -- Pythagorean influence on the harmony of the spheres concept

narrative:
"When Er and the spirits arrived, their duty was to go at once to Lachesis; but first of all there came a prophet who arranged them in order; then he took from the knees of Lachesis lots and samples of lives, and having mounted a high pulpit, spoke as follows:

'Hear the word of Lachesis, the daughter of Necessity. Mortal souls, behold a new cycle of life and mortality. Your genius will not be allotted to you, but you choose your genius; and let him who draws the first lot have the first choice, and the life which he chooses shall be his destiny. Virtue is free, and as a man honours or dishonours her he will have more or less of her; the responsibility is with the chooser -- God is justified.'

When the Interpreter had thus spoken he scattered lots indifferently among them all, and each of them took up the lot which fell near him, all but Er himself (he was not allowed), and each as he took his lot perceived the number which he had obtained.

Then the Interpreter placed on the ground before them the samples of lives; and there were many more lives than the souls present, and they were of all sorts. There were lives of every animal and of man in every condition. And there were tyrannies among them, some lasting out the tyrant's life, others which broke off in the middle and came to an end in poverty and exile and beggary; and there were lives of famous men, some who were famous for their form and beauty as well as for their strength and success in games, or, again, for their birth and the qualities of their ancestors; and some who were the reverse of famous for the opposite qualities. And of women likewise; there was not, however, any definite character in them, because the soul, when choosing a new life, must of necessity become different."
refashioned:
Socrates: When Er and the spirits arrived, they were directed to go immediately to Lachesis. First, a prophet arranged them in order, then took lots and samples of lives from Lachesis's knees. Mounting a high platform, he proclaimed:

"Hear the word of Lachesis, daughter of Necessity! Mortal souls, a new cycle of life and death begins. Your guiding spirit will not be assigned to you -- you will choose your spirit. Whoever draws the first lot chooses first, and the life chosen becomes destiny. Virtue has no master; each person will have more or less of her according to how they honor or dishonor her. The responsibility lies with the chooser. God is not to blame."

The prophet then scattered lots among them randomly. Each soul picked up the lot that fell nearby -- all except Er, who was forbidden to participate. Each soul saw the number on their lot, determining their order of choice.

The prophet laid out samples of lives on the ground before them. There were far more lives than souls, and every imaginable kind was represented: lives of every animal and every human condition. There were tyrannies -- some lasting a full lifetime, others cut short by poverty, exile, and beggary. There were lives of famous men, celebrated for beauty, athletic prowess, noble birth, or ancestral distinction -- and lives of obscurity marked by the opposite qualities. The same variety existed for women's lives. But no life came with a fixed character, because the soul, in choosing a new life, necessarily becomes different.
elegant: A prophet proclaims Lachesis's decree: souls will choose their own guiding spirits and lives. "Virtue has no master -- the responsibility is with the chooser. God is justified." Lots determine the order of choice, not the outcome. Lives of every kind are laid before them -- tyrannies, fame, obscurity, every animal and human condition -- far more lives than souls. No life has a fixed character, because the choosing soul must change.
summary: The prophet of Lachesis announces the central doctrine: souls choose their own lives and guiding spirits. Virtue is free and responsibility belongs to the chooser, not God. Lots determine only the order of choice, and lives of every conceivable kind are available -- but none comes with a fixed character.
distilled: "Virtue is free; the responsibility is with the chooser -- God is justified": souls freely choose their own lives and destinies.
notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating; the Prophet of Lachesis speaking -- "God is justified" (theos anaitios) is one of Plato's most important theological claims -- the problem of evil is solved by free choice -- "Your genius will not be allotted to you, but you choose your genius" (daimon): each soul selects its own daimon/guardian spirit -- The lots determine only order of choice, not destiny itself -- Cross-ref: Republic Book II (379a-380c) on God as not the cause of evil -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 41d-42e on the Demiurge's assignment of souls -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 248c-249b on the cycle of reincarnation and choice -- Cross-ref: Laws 904b-905d on divine justice and personal responsibility

narrative:
"But there was every other quality, and they all mingled with one another, and also with elements of wealth and poverty, and disease and health; and there were mean states also. And here, my dear Glaucon, is the supreme peril of our human state; and therefore the utmost care should be taken. Let each one of us leave every other kind of knowledge and seek and follow one thing only, if peradventure he may be able to learn and may find some one who will make him able to learn and discern between good and evil, and so to choose always and everywhere the better life as he has opportunity.

He should consider the bearing of all these things which have been mentioned severally and collectively upon virtue; he should know what the effect of beauty is when combined with poverty or wealth in a particular soul, and what are the good and evil consequences of noble and humble birth, of private and public station, of strength and weakness, of cleverness and dullness, and of all the soul, and the operation of them when conjoined; he will then look at the nature of the soul, and from the consideration of all these qualities he will be able to determine which is the better and which is the worse; and so he will choose, giving the name of evil to the life which will make his soul more unjust, and good to the life which will make his soul more just; all else he will disregard."
refashioned:
Socrates: Every other quality was mixed in -- wealth and poverty, disease and health, mediocrity and distinction, all blended together.

And here, dear Glaucon, lies the supreme danger of our human condition. This is why the greatest possible care is needed. Let each of us set aside every other kind of knowledge and pursue one thing above all: the ability to discern good from evil, so that we can always choose the better life whenever the opportunity arises.

We should understand how all these qualities -- considered separately and in combination -- affect virtue. What happens when beauty combines with poverty or wealth in a particular soul? What are the consequences of noble versus humble birth, of public versus private life, of strength versus weakness, of cleverness versus dullness? We need to understand how all these qualities interact within the soul. With this understanding, we can determine which life is better and which worse. We should call evil any life that makes the soul more unjust, and good any life that makes the soul more just. Everything else should be disregarded.
elegant: Here lies the supreme peril of human existence: the choice of lives. We must pursue above all else the knowledge that distinguishes good from evil, understanding how every quality -- beauty, wealth, birth, strength, cleverness -- affects the soul's justice. A life that makes the soul more just is good; one that makes it more unjust is evil. All else is irrelevant.
summary: Socrates identifies the supreme human danger: choosing poorly when lives are assigned. The one essential knowledge is discerning good from evil -- understanding how all life qualities affect the soul's justice. A good life makes the soul more just; an evil life makes it more unjust. Everything else is secondary.
distilled: The supreme human task is learning to discern which life will make the soul more just -- all other knowledge is secondary.
notes: Speakers: Socrates (addressing Glaucon directly) -- This passage breaks from mythic narration to direct philosophical exhortation -- "The supreme peril of our human state" connects the myth to practical philosophy -- The emphasis on choosing the life that makes the soul more just brings the entire Republic full circle -- Cross-ref: Republic Book I (352d) on justice as the soul's virtue -- Cross-ref: Republic Book VII (518b-519b) on turning the soul toward the Good -- Cross-ref: Apology 38a ("the unexamined life is not worth living") -- Cross-ref: Meno 87c-89a on virtue as the one essential good

narrative:
"For we have seen and know that this is the best choice both in life and after death. A man must take with him into the world below an adamantine faith in truth and right, that there too he may be undazzled by the desire of wealth or the other allurements of evil, lest, coming upon tyrannies and similar villainies, he do irremediable wrongs to others and suffer yet worse himself; but let him know how to choose the mean and avoid the extremes on either side, as far as possible, not only in this life but in all that which is to come.

For this is the way of happiness."
refashioned:
Socrates: We have seen and know that this is the best choice, both in life and after death. A person must carry into the underworld an unbreakable faith in truth and justice, so that even there he won't be dazzled by the lure of wealth or other enticements of evil. Otherwise, he might choose a tyranny or similar villainy, inflicting irreparable harm on others and suffering worse himself.

The key is knowing how to choose the mean and avoid extremes on either side -- not only in this life but in every life to come.

For this is the way of happiness.
elegant: One must carry into the afterlife an adamantine faith in truth and right, to resist the allure of tyranny, wealth, and other evils when choosing one's next life. Know how to choose the mean and avoid extremes, in this life and all that follow. This is the way of happiness.
summary: Socrates urges carrying an unshakeable commitment to truth and justice even into the afterlife, so that when choosing a new life, one resists the allure of power and wealth. The path to happiness lies in choosing the mean and avoiding extremes across all lifetimes.
distilled: Carry an adamantine faith in justice into every life, choose the mean over extremes, and resist the allure of power -- this is the way of happiness.
notes: Speakers: Socrates -- "Adamantine faith" (adamantinos) -- unbreakable, like the hardest metal -- "Choose the mean and avoid extremes" connects to Aristotle's later Doctrine of the Mean (Nicomachean Ethics II.6) -- "For this is the way of happiness" is the moral lesson of the entire Myth of Er -- The warning against choosing tyranny directly connects to the soul who chose first (10.60) -- Cross-ref: Republic Book IX (571a-580c) on the tyrannical soul as the most miserable -- Cross-ref: Laws 716a on God as the measure and the mean -- Cross-ref: Philebus 64d-66a on the measured life as the best

narrative:
"And according to the report of the messenger from the other world this was what the prophet said at the time: 'Even for the last comer, if he chooses wisely and will live diligently, there is appointed a happy and not undesirable existence. Let not him who chooses first be careless, and let not the last despair.'

And when he had spoken, he who had the first choice came forward and in a moment chose the greatest tyranny; his mind having been darkened by folly and sensuality, he had not thought out the whole matter before he chose, and did not at first sight perceive that he was fated, among other evils, to devour his own children."
refashioned:
Socrates: According to the messenger, the prophet also declared: "Even the last to choose, if he chooses wisely and lives diligently, can have a happy and desirable existence. Let the first to choose not be careless, and let the last not despair."

When he finished speaking, the soul who drew the first lot stepped forward and immediately chose the greatest tyranny. His mind had been darkened by folly and self-indulgence, and he hadn't thought the matter through before choosing. He didn't realize at first that this life included, among other horrors, the fate of devouring his own children.
elegant: The prophet declares that even the last to choose can be happy if he chooses wisely, and the first must not be careless. Yet the soul with the first choice immediately seizes the greatest tyranny, blinded by folly and sensuality, not realizing he has chosen a fate that includes devouring his own children.
summary: The prophet warns against carelessness in choosing, yet the first soul immediately seizes the greatest tyranny, blinded by folly. He fails to see that his chosen life includes devouring his own children -- a devastating illustration of unwise choice.
distilled: Even with the first choice, folly leads to ruin: the soul seizes tyranny without realizing he has chosen to devour his own children.
notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating; the Prophet speaking -- "Even for the last comer" -- the lot determines order, not fate; wisdom matters more than position -- "Devour his own children" likely alludes to mythic tyrants like Thyestes or Kronos -- The first chooser's folly exemplifies exactly the danger Socrates warned against -- Cross-ref: Republic Book IX (571c-576b) on the tyrannical soul consumed by appetites -- Cross-ref: Republic Book VIII (565d-566a) on the tyrant's transformation -- Cross-ref: Herodotus I.108-130 on Astyages and the myth of kings consuming their children's flesh

narrative:
"But when he had time to reflect, and saw what was in the lot, he began to beat his breast and lament over his choice, forgetting the proclamation of the prophet; for, instead of throwing the blame of his misfortune on himself, he accused chance and the gods, and everything rather than himself.

Now he was one of those who came from heaven, and in a former life had dwelt in a well-ordered State, but his virtue was a matter of habit only, and he had no philosophy. And it was true of others who were similarly overtaken, that the greater number of them came from heaven and therefore they had never been schooled by trial, whereas the pilgrims who came from earth, having themselves suffered and seen others suffer, were not in a hurry to choose.

And owing to this inexperience of theirs, and also because the lot was a chance, many of the souls exchanged a good destiny for an evil or an evil for a good."
refashioned:
Socrates: But when he had time to reflect and saw what his choice actually contained, he beat his breast and wailed over his decision, completely forgetting the prophet's proclamation. Instead of blaming himself for his misfortune, he accused luck, the gods, everything except himself.

This soul had come from heaven. In his previous life he had lived in a well-ordered state, but his virtue was merely habitual -- he had no philosophy. This was true of many others who made similar mistakes: most of them came from heaven and had never been tested by suffering. The souls who came from earth, having suffered themselves and watched others suffer, were far more cautious in their choosing.

Because of this inexperience, and because the lottery was random, many souls traded a good destiny for an evil one, or an evil for a good.
elegant: The first chooser, discovering his fate, wailed and blamed the gods -- anyone but himself. He had come from heaven, where his virtue was habitual, not philosophical. Most who chose poorly came from heaven, untested by suffering. The earth-worn souls, schooled by hardship, chose more carefully. Inexperience and the randomness of the lots caused many to exchange good destinies for evil ones.
summary: The first chooser, realizing his terrible mistake, blames the gods rather than himself. He had lived virtuously by habit in a previous life but lacked philosophical understanding. Those from heaven, untested by suffering, generally chose poorly, while souls who had suffered on earth chose more wisely.
distilled: Habitual virtue without philosophy fails the ultimate test -- only those schooled by suffering and guided by wisdom choose well.
notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- "His virtue was a matter of habit only, and he had no philosophy" -- this is one of the most important lines in the Republic, distinguishing genuine philosophical virtue from mere habituation -- It validates the entire Republic's project: philosophical education, not just good habits, is necessary for true virtue -- Those from heaven (who lived good lives) are more vulnerable than those from earth (who suffered) -- Cross-ref: Republic Book VII (514a-521b) on the necessity of philosophical education beyond mere opinion -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 82a-b on the distinction between philosophical and customary virtue -- Cross-ref: Meno 97a-98b on the instability of true opinion without knowledge

narrative:
"For if a man had always on his arrival in this world dedicated himself from the first to sound philosophy, and had been moderately fortunate in the number of the lot, he might, as the messenger reported, be happy here, and also his journey to another life and return to this, instead of being rough and underground, would be smooth and heavenly.

Most curious, he said, was the spectacle -- sad and laughable and strange; for the choice of the souls was in most cases based on their experience of a previous life. There he saw the soul which had once been Orpheus choosing the life of a swan out of enmity to the race of women, hating to be born of a woman because they had been his murderers; he beheld also the soul of Thamyras choosing the life of a nightingale; birds, on the other hand, like the swan and other musicians, wanting to be men.

The soul which obtained the twentieth lot chose the life of a lion, and this was the soul of Ajax the son of Telamon, who would not be a man, remembering the injustice which was done him in the judgment about the arms."
refashioned:
Socrates: If a person had always dedicated himself to genuine philosophy from the start, and been reasonably fortunate in the lottery, he could be happy in this world, and his passage between lives -- rather than being rough and underground -- would be smooth and heavenly.

The spectacle of the choosing, Er reported, was extraordinary -- at once sad, laughable, and strange. Most souls based their choices on their previous life's experiences.

He saw the soul that had once been Orpheus choose the life of a swan, out of hatred for the race of women -- since women had murdered him, he refused to be born from a woman. The soul of Thamyras chose the life of a nightingale. Meanwhile, birds like swans and other musical creatures wanted to become human.

The soul that drew the twentieth lot chose the life of a lion. This was Ajax, son of Telamon, who refused to be a man again, still remembering the injustice done to him in the judgment over Achilles' armor.
elegant: Philosophy ensures happiness across lifetimes and smooth passage between them. The choosing spectacle was sad, laughable, and strange -- souls chose based on previous experience. Orpheus, murdered by women, chose to be a swan rather than be born from one. Thamyras became a nightingale. Ajax, still bitter over the judgment of Achilles' arms, refused to be human again and chose a lion's life.
summary: Philosophy guarantees happiness across lifetimes. The choosing was a strange spectacle: Orpheus chose to be a swan out of hatred for women who killed him; Thamyras became a nightingale; Ajax chose a lion's life, still bitter about the unjust judgment over Achilles' armor.
distilled: Souls choose their next lives based on past experience -- philosophy alone ensures wise choosing across all lifetimes.
notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- Orpheus: legendary musician torn apart by Maenads (Bacchantes/women of Thrace) -- Thamyras (Thamyris): mythic poet/musician who challenged the Muses and was blinded (Iliad II.594-600) -- Ajax, son of Telamon: Greek hero who went mad and killed himself after the armor of the dead Achilles was awarded to Odysseus instead (dramatized in Sophocles' Ajax) -- The Ajax story is also told in Odyssey 11.543-567, where Ajax's shade refuses to speak to Odysseus -- Cross-ref: Odyssey 11.543-567 (Ajax in the underworld) -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 248d-249b on the cycle of reincarnation -- Cross-ref: Republic Book III (386b-387c) on heroic behavior in the face of death

narrative:
"The next was Agamemnon, who took the life of an eagle, because, like Ajax, he hated human nature by reason of his sufferings. About the middle came the lot of Atalanta; she, seeing the great fame of an athlete, was unable to resist the temptation: and after her there followed the soul of Epeus the son of Panopeus passing into the nature of a woman cunning in the arts; and far away among the last who chose, the soul of the jester Thersites was putting on the form of a monkey.

There came also the soul of Odysseus having yet to make a choice, and his lot happened to be the last of them all."
refashioned:
Socrates: Next came Agamemnon, who chose the life of an eagle -- like Ajax, he had grown to hate human nature because of his sufferings. Around the middle of the choosing, Atalanta's lot came up. Seeing the great glory attached to an athlete's life, she couldn't resist the temptation. After her, the soul of Epeus, son of Panopeus, passed into the nature of a woman skilled in the arts. Far toward the end, among the last to choose, the soul of the buffoon Thersites was putting on the form of a monkey.

Then came the soul of Odysseus, who still had to make his choice. His lot happened to be the very last of all.
elegant: Agamemnon, hating humanity after his sufferings, chose an eagle. Atalanta couldn't resist an athlete's fame. Epeus became a craftswoman. Thersites became a monkey. Last of all came Odysseus, with the final lot.
summary: More famous souls make their choices: Agamemnon becomes an eagle out of hatred for humanity, Atalanta is drawn to athletic fame, Epeus becomes a craftswoman, and Thersites becomes a monkey. Odysseus, with the very last lot, has yet to choose.
distilled: Each famous soul's choice reflects its previous life's experience and character -- Agamemnon an eagle, Atalanta an athlete, Thersites a monkey -- with Odysseus choosing last.
notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- Agamemnon: king of Mycenae, leader of the Greek army at Troy, murdered by his wife Clytemnestra on his return -- Atalanta: mythic huntress and athlete, known for racing her suitors -- Epeus: builder of the Trojan Horse (Odyssey 8.493, 11.523); his choice of a craftswoman's life fits his artisan nature -- Thersites: the ugly, insolent commoner in the Iliad (II.211-277), beaten by Odysseus -- The monkey choice for Thersites is comic and fitting -- Cross-ref: Iliad II.211-277 (Thersites) -- Cross-ref: Odyssey 11.405-434 (Agamemnon's shade tells Odysseus of his murder) -- Cross-ref: Apollodorus, Library III.9.2 (Atalanta's footrace)

narrative:
"Now the recollection of former toils had disenchanted him of ambition, and he went about for a considerable time in search of the life of a private man who had no cares; he had some difficulty in finding this, which was lying about and had been neglected by everybody else; and when he saw it, he said that he would have done the same had his lot been first instead of last, and that he was delighted to have it.

And not only did men pass into animals, but I must also mention that there were animals tame and wild who changed into one another and into corresponding human natures -- the good into the gentle and the evil into the savage, in all sorts of combinations."
refashioned:
Socrates: The memory of his former struggles had cured Odysseus of all ambition. He spent a long time searching for the life of an ordinary private person free from cares. He had difficulty finding it -- it was lying off to the side, ignored by everyone else. When he found it, he said he would have chosen the same life even if he'd drawn the first lot instead of the last, and he was delighted with it.

I should also mention that the transformations went in every direction: not only did human souls pass into animal forms, but tame and wild animals changed into one another and into human natures -- the good becoming gentle creatures and the evil becoming savage ones, in every possible combination.
elegant: Odysseus, cured of ambition by his past sufferings, searched long for the life of a quiet, carefree private man. He found it neglected by everyone else, and said he'd have chosen it even with the first lot. Souls also crossed between human and animal forms in every direction -- good into gentle, evil into savage.
summary: Odysseus, his ambition extinguished by past hardships, deliberately chooses the quiet life of an ordinary private citizen -- a life ignored by everyone else. He declares he would have chosen the same even with the first pick. Souls also transformed freely between human and animal forms.
distilled: Odysseus, wisened by suffering, chooses the quiet life of an obscure private man over all the glories of the world -- and would have chosen it first or last.
notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- Odysseus's choice is the moral climax of the Myth of Er -- The man of action and cunning chooses obscurity over glory -- This resonates with Socrates' own preference for the examined private life over political ambition -- The neglected life "lying about" suggests that the best choice is often overlooked -- Cross-ref: Odyssey 11.488-491 where Achilles' shade tells Odysseus he'd rather be a living slave than king of the dead -- Cross-ref: Republic Book VII (519c-521b) on philosophers preferring contemplation to political power -- Cross-ref: Apology 36b-37a on Socrates' choice of private philosophical life

refashioned:
Socrates: Once all souls had chosen, they proceeded in the order of their choice to Lachesis. She assigned each soul the guiding spirit it had chosen, to serve as guardian and fulfiller of its destiny. This spirit led each soul first to Clotho, drawing it into the revolution of the spindle driven by her hand, thus ratifying the chosen destiny. Then it carried the soul to Atropos, who spun the threads and made them irreversible. From there, without looking back, they passed beneath the throne of Necessity.

Then they all marched through scorching heat across the Plain of Forgetfulness -- a barren wasteland without trees or greenery. Toward evening they camped by the River of Unmindfulness, whose water no vessel can contain. Every soul was required to drink a certain amount. Those not preserved by wisdom drank far more than necessary, and each one forgot everything as they drank.

After they lay down to rest, around midnight a thunderstorm and earthquake struck. In an instant, the souls were hurled upward in all directions toward their births, like shooting stars.

Er himself was prevented from drinking the water. How he returned to his body he couldn't say. He only knew that in the morning he awoke suddenly and found himself lying on the funeral pyre.
elegant: Each soul received its chosen guardian spirit, was ratified by Clotho, made irreversible by Atropos, and passed beneath Necessity's throne. Then they crossed the scorching Plain of Forgetfulness to the River of Unmindfulness, whose water erases all memory. Those without wisdom drank too deeply and forgot everything. At midnight, a thunderstorm launched them toward rebirth like shooting stars. Er alone was forbidden to drink and returned to his body, waking on the pyre.
summary: After choosing, each soul receives its guardian spirit and has its destiny ratified by the Fates. They cross the Plain of Forgetfulness and drink from the River of Unmindfulness -- the unwise drinking deeply and forgetting everything. At midnight they are launched toward rebirth. Er alone, forbidden to drink, returns to his body and wakes on the funeral pyre.
distilled: The chosen destinies are sealed by the Fates, then all memory is erased by the River of Unmindfulness -- except for Er, who returns to tell the tale.
notes: Speakers: Socrates narrating -- The three Fates complete their roles: Lachesis (allots), Clotho (ratifies), Atropos (makes irreversible) -- The Plain of Forgetfulness (Lethe pedion) and River of Unmindfulness (Ameles potamos) echo the Greek underworld geography -- "Those not saved by wisdom drank more" -- philosophy preserves memory/understanding even across incarnations -- Souls launched "like shooting stars" is a vivid image of rebirth -- Er's exemption from drinking allows the tale to be told -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 248c-249b on the role of forgetfulness in the cycle of reincarnation -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 72e-77d on recollection (anamnesis) as evidence of previous knowledge -- Cross-ref: Virgil's Aeneid VI.713-751 (the River Lethe, clearly influenced by this passage)

narrative: "And thus, Glaucon, the tale has been saved and has not perished, and will save us if we are obedient to the word spoken; and we shall pass safely over the river of Forgetfulness and our soul will not be defiled. Wherefore my counsel is that we hold fast ever to the heavenly way and follow after justice and virtue always, considering that the soul is immortal and able to endure every sort of good and every sort of evil. Thus shall we live dear to one another and to the gods, both while remaining here and when, like conquerors in the games who go round to gather gifts, we receive our reward. And it shall be well with us both in this life and in the pilgrimage of a thousand years which we have been describing."
refashioned:
Socrates: And so, Glaucon, the tale has been saved and has not perished -- and it will save us, if we heed its message. We shall pass safely over the River of Forgetfulness and our souls will not be corrupted.

My counsel is this: hold fast always to the heavenly way. Pursue justice and virtue in everything, remembering that the soul is immortal and able to endure every form of good and evil. In this way we shall live beloved by one another and by the gods, both during our time here and when -- like victorious athletes making their rounds to collect prizes -- we receive our reward.

And it shall be well with us, both in this life and in the pilgrimage of a thousand years we have been describing.
elegant: The tale is saved and will save us if we heed it. Hold fast to the heavenly way, pursue justice and virtue always, knowing the soul is immortal. So shall we live dear to each other and to the gods, and receive our reward like victorious athletes. It shall be well with us in this life and in the thousand-year pilgrimage to come.
summary: Socrates concludes the Republic with a call to hold fast to justice and virtue, knowing the soul is immortal. The tale of Er will save us if we heed it: we shall live beloved by gods and men, and receive our reward both in this life and across the thousand-year journey of the soul.
distilled: Hold fast to the heavenly way, pursue justice always, and it shall be well with us in this life and in the thousand-year pilgrimage of the immortal soul.
notes: Speakers: Socrates addressing Glaucon -- This is the final passage of the Republic -- "The tale has been saved" (mythos esothe) puns on salvation through story/myth -- "Like conquerors in the games" returns to the athletic metaphor from 10.49 -- "The pilgrimage of a thousand years" refers to the cycle described in the Myth of Er -- The closing exhortation unites all the Republic's themes: justice, virtue, the immortal soul, divine reward, the good life -- Cross-ref: Republic Book I (352d) where the question of justice first arose -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 114d-115a for a similar closing exhortation about the soul's fate -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 527a-e for a parallel closing appeal to live justly -- Cross-ref: Laws 715e-716b on following God and the heavenly way
